answer:
Yeah, so, the last group project I did in school was actually for my psychology class. We had to create a presentation about how social media impacts teenagers—like, both the positive and negative sides. To be honest, at first, working with a group kind of made me nervous because you never really know if everyone’s going to do their part, right? But, well, it turned out surprisingly well. Each of us had different strengths: one person was great at research, another was super organized and kept us on track, and I ended up taking charge of designing the slides because I’m pretty creative. We started by discussing what aspects of social media we thought were most relevant, then assigned ourselves sections to work on individually. You know, sometimes people disagreed about which examples to use, but we managed those moments by listening and trying to compromise. Actually, I found that being open to others’ ideas—even if they challenged mine—made the final presentation a lot more balanced and persuasive.

Throughout the process, I realized that I’m not just comfortable taking the lead, but I'm also capable of adapting when unexpected issues come up, like someone needing more time or help. I guess I kind of learned that collaboration is really about flexibility and accountability. For example, when someone forgot to send their part on time, instead of getting frustrated, we just worked together to fix it. Overall, the experience taught me that I can trust others, and that working as a team can be genuinely motivating. So now, I feel a lot more confident about partnering with people in the future, especially when tackling complex projects.
title: Describe the last group project you completed in school. How did the group members work together to complete the tasks? What did you learn about yourself while working together with others?
